Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - AQUILA: COMMUNICATION EFFICIENT FEDER ATED LEARNING WITH ADAPTIVE QUANTIZATION OF LAZILY-AGGREGATED GRADIENTS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The development and deployment of federated learning (FL) have been bottlenecked by the heavy communication overheads of high-dimensional models between the distributed client nodes and the central server. To achieve better errorcommunication tradeoffs, recent efforts have been made to either adaptively reduce the communication frequency by skipping unimportant updates, a.k.a. lazilyaggregated quantization (LAQ), or adjust the quantization bits for each communication. In this paper, we propose a unifying communication efficient framework for FL based on **a** daptive **qu** ant **i** zation of **l** azily- **a** ggregated gradients ( **AQUILA** ), which adaptively adjusts two mutually-dependent factors, the communication frequency and the quantization level, in a synergistic way. Specifically, we start from a careful investigation on the classical LAQ scheme and formulate AQUILA as an optimization problem where the optimal quantization level per communication is selected by minimizing the gradient loss caused by updates skipping. Meanwhile, we adjust the LAQ strategy to better fit the novel quantization criterion and thus keep the communication frequency at an appropriate level. The effectiveness and convergence of the proposed AQUILA framework are theoretically verified. The experimental results demonstrate that AQUILA can reduce around **50%** of overall transmitted bits compared to existing methods while achieving the same level of model accuracy in a number of non-homogeneous FL scenarios, including Non-IID data distribution and heterogeneous model architecture. The proposed AQUILA is highly adaptive and compatible to existing FL settings.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

With the deployment of ubiquitous sensing and computing devices, the Internet of things (IoT) as well as many other distributed systems have gradually grown from concept to reality, bringing dramatic convenience to people’s daily life (Du et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020a; Hard et al., 2018). To fully utilize such distributed computing resources, distributed learning provides a promising framework that can achieve comparable performance with the traditional centralized learning scheme. However, the privacy and security of sensitive data during the updating and transmitting processes in distributed learning have been a growing concern. In this context, _federated learning (FL)_ (McMahan et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019) has been developed, enabling distributed devices to collaboratively learn a global model without privacy leakage by keeping private data sets isolated and masking transmitted information with secure approaches like differential privacy (Abadi et al., 2016), secret sharing techniques (Bonawitz et al., 2017) and homomorphic encryption (Liu et al., 2020b). Due to its privacy and security preserving property and great potentials in some distributed but privacysensitive fields like finance and health, FL has attracted tremendous attentions from both academia and industry in recent years.

Unfortunately, in many FL applications like image classification and objective recognition, the models to be trained tend to be high-dimensional, which lead to heavy communication overheads, for example, a Resnet-152 network has over 58 million parameters (He et al., 2016). Hence, communication efficiency has become one of the key bottlenecks of FL. To this end, recent researches have tried to reduce the communication frequency, for example, Sun et al. (2020) proposes the lazily-aggregated quantization (LAQ) method to reduce communication rounds by skipping some

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

unnecessary parameter uploads. To further reduce transmitted bits per communication, LAQ can be used jointly with gradient compression techniques, e.g. quantization and sparsification (Strom, 2015; Wangni et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2018; Han et al., 2020). Moreover, Mao et al. (2021) develops the Adaptive Quantized Gradient (AQG) for LAQ to adjust the quantization bit among multiple given levels during training. However, the AQG is not sufficiently adaptive, for example, in the two-level AQG with 4 bit and 2 bit, the situation of 3 bit and 1 bit is not covered at all. In a separate line of work, Jhunjhunwala et al. (2021) develops an adaptive quantization rule (AdaQuantFL) for FL which can search in a given range for an optimal quantization level and achieve a better error-communication tradeoff.

Previous work has investigated how to optimize communication frequency or adjust quantization levels in a highly adaptive fashion, but not both. Intuitively, we ask the question, _can we adaptively adjust the quantization level in LAQ to further reduce communication rounds and transmitted bits simultaneously?_ A straightforward approach is to train LAQ jointly with state-of-the-art adaptive quantization methods like AdaQuantFL. However, quantization level and communication frequency are mutually dependent in establishing the model convergence and must be adjusted together. For example, lazy aggregation leads to the skip of some gradients that no longer require quantization, while the choice of quantization levels directly affects the quality of a gradient update and thus whether it is selected for transmission and aggregation. Therefore, the key question of our work is how to jointly leverage these two complementary yet mutually-dependent degrees of freedom for further optimizing communication efficiency in FL.

The key idea of this paper is to select the optimal quantization bits for each communication round in LAQ by optimizing the gradient loss caused by skipping quantized updates, which gives a novel quantization criterion that can cooperate with LAQ strategy to further reduce overall transmitted bits while maintaining the desired convergence properties of LAQ. The contributions of this paper are trifold and summarized as follows. 1) We propose a FL framework with **a** daptive **qu** ant **i** zation of **l** azily- **a** ggregated gradients termed AQUILA, which simultaneously adjusts the communication frequency and quantization level in a synergistic fashion. 2) We formulate AQUILA as an optimization problem and develop an upperbound for the gradient loss caused by communication skipping, which gives a novel adaptive quantization criterion which is theoretically proven to be more efficient compared to AdaQuantFL while maintaining the same convergence properties. 3) We experimentally evaluate the performance of AQUILA in a number of non-homogeneous FL settings, including Non-IID data distribution and various heterogeneous model architecture. The experimental results show that AQUILA can significantly mitigate the communication overhead over a number of baselines including fixed-bit LAQ and the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL. Our approach is highly adaptive and compatible to existing FL settings.

Recently, AdaQuantFL is proposed to achieve a better error-communication tradeoff by adaptively adjusting the quantization levels during FL training (Jhunjhunwala et al., 2021). Specifically, AdaQuantFL computes iteration _k_ ’s optimal quantization level _b[∗] k_[based on the following criterion] involving training loss and initial quantization level _b_ 0:
where _f_ ( _**θ**_[0] ) and _f_ ( _**θ**[k]_ ) are the global training loss at iteration 0 and _k_ , respectively.

However, AdaQuantFL transmits quantized gradients at **every iteration** . In order to skip unnecessary communication rounds and adaptively adjust quantization level for each communication jointly, an naive approach is to quantize lazily aggregated gradients with AdaQuantFL. Nevertheless, it fails to achieve efficient communication due to a number of reasons. Firstly, given the descending trend of training loss, AdaQuantFL’s criterion (6) may lead to high quantization bit number even exceeding 32 late in the training process, which is too much for cases where the global convergence is already approaching. Secondly, higher quantization level results in smaller quantization error, which will lead to lower communication threshold in LAQ’s criterion (4) and thus higher frequency of transmission. Therefore it is desirable to develop more efficient adaptive quantization method in the lazily-aggregated setting to systematically improve communication efficiency in FL.

## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 ADAPTIVE QUANTIZATION OF LAZILY-AGGREGATED GRADIENTS (AQUILA)

Given the above limitations of the naive joint use of existing adaptive quantization criterion and lazy aggregation strategy, this paper aims to design a unifying framework for communication efficiency optimization where the quantization level and communication frequency are adjusted in a synergistic and interactive way. Based on a careful investigation on LAQ, we design a novel quantization criterion where the optimal quantization level is selected by minimizing the expected gradient loss caused by skipping quantized updates. The rationale behind such strategy is, by formulating the adaptive quantization problem of lazily-aggregated gradients as optimizing the expected gradient loss with respect to the number of quantization bits, we can get an adaptive quantization criterion based on local gradient updates while maintaining or even improving the convergence property of

The superiority of (7) comes from the following two aspects. Firstly, the gradient updates tend to fluctuate along with the training process instead of keeping descending like the loss value, and thus prevent the quantization level from increasing tremendously compared with the initial level. Secondly, with lazy aggregation criterion based on gradient updates like (4), the transmitted bits in AQUILA is further controlled, since the gradient update for actual transmission in (7) is lower bounded by the lazy aggregation criterion, and therefore high-bit transmission for small update is more likely to be skipped.

Figure 1: The schematic illustration of the communication efficient FL with AQUILA in comparison with the naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ. The blue lines indicating the transmission of quantized gradients in AQUILA are drawn in different thicknesses to represent different quantization levels selected by various clients.

The cooperation of the novel adaptive quantization criterion (7) and the modified lazy aggregation strategy (8) is illustrated in Fig. 1a. Compared to the naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ where the mutual influence between adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation has not been considered as shown in Fig. 1b, our AQUILA framework adaptively optimizes the allocation of quantization bits throughout training to promote the convergence of lazy aggregation, and at the same time utilizes the lazy aggregation strategy to improve the efficiency of adaptive quantization by skipping high-bit transmission. The proposed AQUILA’s effect of suppressing the transmission of high quantization bits has been verified in our experiments, as shown in Fig. 20 in the Appendix. Besides, with the adjusted lazy aggregation strategy (8), AQUILA well addresses the problem of high communication frequency in the late training process of naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ, as indicated by Fig. 2.

The proposed AQUILA is summarized as follows in Algorithm 1. At iteration _k_ = 0, all clients are forced to transmit local gradients quantized with the initial level _b_ 0. At iteration _k ∈{_ 1 _,_ 2 _, ..., K}_ , the server first broadcasts the global model _**θ**[k]_ to all clients. Each client _m_ computes _**g**[k] m_[with] local training data, and then uses it to select an optimal quantization level by (7). Then, each client

## 3.2 THEORETICAL DERIVATION AND ANALYSIS OF AQUILA

## 4 EXPERIMENTS AND DISCUSSION

## 4.1 EXPERIMENT SETUP

**Dataset.** In this paper, we evaluate our method with MNIST and CIFAR10 dataset, considering both IID and Non-IID data distribution. To simulate Non-IID situation, each client is assigned with two classes of data at most and the amount of data for each class is balanced.

**Parameters.** We set total client number _M_ = 10, and follow the settings in LAQ, where _D_ = 10 and _ξ_ 1 = _ξ_ 2 = _· · ·_ = _ξD_ = 0 _._ 8 _/D_ . In terms of initial quantization level, a low level _b_ 0 = 2 is chosen for simpler tasks with MNIST, and a larger level _b_ 0 = 6 is selected for more complex tasks with CIFAR10. For both AdaQuantFL and AQUILA, we set an upperbound for quantization level as _bmax_ = 16 in case that the level grows too high.
**Training.** We train a CNN with MNIST and a Resnet18 network with CIFAR10. The hyperparameters of our experiments are shown in Table 1 in Appendix.

We first evaluate our proposed AQUILA with homogeneous settings where all the local models share the same architecture as the global model. The performance of AQUILA is compared with several state-of-the-art methods including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), QSGD (Alistarh et al., 2017), AdaQuantFL, fixed-bit LAQ and the naive combination of AdaQuantFL with LAQ. For the choice of quantization level for fixed-bit LAQ, since AQUILA’s initial level _b_ 0 is 2 for MNIST and 6 for CIFAR10, we compare AQUILA with LAQ-2 and LAQ-6 for MNIST and CIFAR 10 respectively. Besides, the performance of LAQ with the upperbound _bmax_ = 16 is also evaluated.

We also evaluate our proposed AQUILA with HeteroFL (Diao et al., 2020), where the local models trained at clients’ side are heterogeneous. ## 4.2 PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS

In this part, we analyze the performance of AQUILA with various experiment settings including Non-IID data distribution and heterogeneous model architecture. From figures in this paper, we can observe that:

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

- AQUILA achieves a significant transmission reduction on both MNIST and CIFAR10 as compared to AdaQuantFL and the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL. For instance, AQUILA saves 65.98% of transmitted bits compared with AdaQuantFL and 62.05% compared with the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL in the IID scenario with CIFAR10 dataset as shown in Fig. 3d, and other figures all show an obvious reduction in terms of the total transmitted bits required for convergence. Fig. 4 verifies that the quantization level selected by AQUILA will not continuously increase during training like AdaQuantFL and LAQ with AdaQuantFL.

- Similarly, AQUILA outperforms all fixed-level LAQ in terms of overall transmitted bits for both MNIST and CIFAR10, as shown in Fig. 3. For example, Fig. 3a indicates that AQUILA reduces 42.6% of total transmitted bits compared with LAQ-2 and 92.32% compared with LAQ-16 in MNIST. The transmission reduction is 38.76% for LAQ-6 and 78.09% for LAQ-16 in CIFAR10 as shown in Fig. 3e. Besides, Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 indicate that although LAQ with fixed but low quantization level like LAQ-2 and LAQ-4 sometimes transmit smaller amount of bits per step compared with AQUILA, they suffer from lower accuracy and slower convergence. It further verifies the necessity and effectiveness of our well-designed adaptive quantization criterion which achieves fast convergence with similar low-bit transmission but without degradation of the model performance.

- AQUILA also works well with heterogeneous local models. With the two various ways of distributing the heterogeneous local models in this paper, our proposed AQUILA still outperforms other methods by significantly reducing overall transmitted bits while maintaining the same convergence property and model accuracy. Please refer to Fig. 10 to Fig. 19 in the Appendix for more detailed information. These experimental results in nonhomogeneous FL settings prove that our proposed AQUILA can be used in a more general and complicated federated learning scenarios.

## 5 CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORK

This paper proposes a communication efficient FL framework to simultaneously adjust two mutually-dependent degrees of freedom: communication frequency and quantization level. With the close cooperation of the novel adaptive quantization and adjusted lazy aggregation strategy developed in this paper, the proposed AQUILA has been proven to be capable of reducing the transmitted bits while maintaining the same convergence property and model performance compared against existing methods both theoretically and experimentally. The evaluation with Non-IID data distribution and various heterogeneous model architectures demonstrates that AQUILA is compatible to existing FL settings. Future works include further improvements and theoretical guarantee for FL systems with heterogeneity.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper addresses the critical bottleneck of communication overhead in Federated Learning (FL). The proposed framework, AQUILA, is well-motivated by the observation that communication frequency (lazy aggregation) and communication precision (quantization) are typically treated as orthogonal problems, despite being mathematically interdependent. The clarity of the introduction is commendable; it successfully identifies a gap in existing literature—specifically the lack of a synergistic approach to these two variables—and clearly articulates the "trifold" contributions. The theoretical ambition of minimizing "gradient loss caused by updates skipping" to determine quantization levels is a sophisticated pivot from standard heuristics. However, the paper’s primary challenge lies in the complexity of its proposed coupling. As noted in the introduction, the "mutually-dependent" nature of these factors suggests that a poorly tuned AQUILA could potentially lead to training instabilities or "stale" gradients that prevent convergence in highly non-IID settings. While the abstract claims a 50% reduction in transmitted bits, the reviewer will be looking for rigorous proof that the computational overhead of calculating the "optimal" quantization bit-width doesn't offset the communication gains, and whether the framework remains robust across diverse, high-dimensional architectures where the gradient distributions may vary significantly. Overall, this is a strong, well-structured proposal that tackles a high-impact problem with a novel, unified perspective.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents **AQUILA**, a novel and well-motivated framework for improving communication efficiency in federated learning by synergistically combining adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation—two previously studied but independently optimized dimensions of compression. The writing is generally clear, the problem is timely, and the technical motivation is strong: the mutual dependence between communication frequency and quantization level is convincingly argued, and the proposed solution—formulating adaptive bit selection as an optimization of gradient loss induced by skipped updates—is both intuitive and theoretically grounded. The paper benefits from a solid structure, with a clear progression from problem identification to method design, theoretical analysis, and empirical validation. However, the most significant issue lies in the **lack of full methodological clarity in the core algorithmic contribution**, particularly in how exactly the adaptive quantization criterion is integrated into the lazy aggregation mechanism. While the abstract and introduction emphasize the "synergistic" adjustment of both factors, the manuscript (at least in the provided portion) stops short of clearly articulating the joint optimization loop—specifically, how the quantization level is dynamically updated in response to aggregation decisions (and vice versa) in a feedback-aware manner. This opacity raises concerns about reproducibility and conceptual novelty, especially given prior work like AdaQuantFL and AQG. Additionally, while theoretical justification is claimed, the truncated content prevents assessment of the rigor and assumptions underlying the convergence guarantees. On the empirical side, the reported 50% reduction in transmitted bits is promising, but without seeing the full experimental setup and baseline comparisons (e.g., ablation between adaptive quantization, lazy aggregation, and their combination), it is difficult to attribute gains definitively to the proposed synergy. In sum, the paper shows strong potential in addressing a critical bottleneck in FL with an elegant unifying idea, but its current form requires greater algorithmic transparency and completion of technical exposition to fully validate its claims of superiority and coherence.

comments.title: Incorrect norm in quantizer definition: L2 norm used instead of L-infinity
comments.explanation: The paper defines the stochastic uniform quantizer based on Alistarh et al. (2017), which uses the L-infinity norm \|g\|_\infty for normalization to ensure each coordinate is scaled within [-\|g\|_\infty, \|g\|_\infty]. However, the text specifies normalization using the L2 norm, i.e., |g_i| / \|g\|_2, which is inconsistent. This affects the dynamic range of the quantization bins and violates the theoretical properties of the quantizer. Multiple occurrences of this inconsistency appear across sections (e.g., bin definition, quantization formula), suggesting it is an author error rather than OCR. The intended behavior requires \|g\|_\infty, and using \|g\|_2 distorts the scaling, impacting quantization variance and downstream analysis, such as bit cost and error bounds.

comments.title: Incorrect bit count formula: uses \lceil \log_2(b+1) \rceil instead of \lceil \log_2 b \rceil
comments.quote: C_b = d \lceil \log_2( b + 1 ) \rceil + d + 32
comments.explanation: The communication cost formula assumes each quantized coordinate index requires \lceil \log_2(b+1) \rceil bits, but in b-level stochastic quantization, only b distinct levels are used (l \in \{0, 1, ..., b-1\}), requiring \lceil \log_2 b \rceil bits. This is a technical inaccuracy: b+1 levels would imply b+1 possible indices. The cited work (Alistarh et al., 2017) uses b levels and \lceil \log_2 b \rceil bits. The error, though small for large b, misrepresents the bit cost model and propagates into claimed '50% reduction' comparisons, potentially overstating efficiency gains. Correcting to \lceil \log_2 b \rceil aligns with standard quantization practices.

comments.quote: we can get an adaptive quantization criterion (7) that selects the quantization level for client m at iteration k based on initial quantization level b0 and the change between client m’s newly computed gradient g_m[k] and the last uploaded gradient \hat{g}_m[k−1]
comments.explanation: Criterion (7) is referenced multiple times but never displayed, likely due to OCR or formatting loss. The verbal description suggests dependence on gradient change and b0, but the later derivation from the upper bound (9) implies dependence on global or unknown constants (e.g., \sigma, d_m). This creates a contradiction: is (7) locally computable using only client-side information, or does it depend on centralized parameters? Furthermore, Algorithm 1 relies on (7) to compute b_m[k], undermining reproducibility. The absence of (7) and the mismatch between description and derivation raise serious concerns about the method's transparency and implementability.

comments.title: Ambiguous and potentially flawed derivation of adaptive quantization criterion from upper bound
comments.quote: the optimal b[k]_m is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to zero... dividing b[k]_m by b[1]_m... gives the adaptive selection criterion in (7)
comments.explanation: The paper claims that normalizing the optimal b_m[k] by its initial value b_m[1] removes dependence on unknown constants (e.g., \sigma, d_m). However, without showing (9) or its derivative, this step appears unjustified. If b_m[k] depends on gradient norms, L-smoothness, or quantization variance (q_b), then b_m[1] inherits the same dependencies, so their ratio does not necessarily eliminate unknowns. Moreover, q_b itself depends on b, introducing a circularity. The argument skips non-trivial analysis and gives the impression of algebraic sleight-of-hand. This undermines the theoretical basis for criterion (7) and affects the claimed independence from inaccessible parameters.

comments.explanation: Both the original lazy aggregation condition (4) and its modified version (8) are absent from the text, with garbled OCR-like fragments suggesting truncation. The authors emphasize that (8) avoids expansion of skipped client groups under heterogeneous quantization, but without its form, it is impossible to assess correctness, stability, or theoretical validity. Since the algorithm's convergence and client participation depend on these conditions, their absence critically hinders evaluation and reproducibility.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of AdaQuantFL's communication and quantization behavior
comments.quote: AdaQuantFL transmits quantized gradients at **every iteration**
comments.explanation: The paper inaccurately portrays AdaQuantFL as transmitting at every iteration, implying a fixed communication schedule. However, AdaQuantFL (Jhunjhunwala et al., 2021) supports variable communication and sparsification. Similarly, the claim that AdaQuantFL's quantization levels 'exceed 32' is mischaracterized: increasing bit precision late in training is a deliberate strategy to improve convergence, not a flaw. The paper frames these as inefficiencies to justify AQUILA's novelty, but this misrepresentation skews the baseline comparison and weakens the motivation for the proposed method.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of quantization level in lazy aggregation mechanism
comments.quote: Set \hat{g}[k]_m = \hat{g}_m[k−1] and \hat{b}[k]_m = \hat{b}[k]_m[−]1 on both sides
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 computes a new b_m[k] every iteration via criterion (7), but only updates \hat{b}_m[k] if the client uploads. Otherwise, it reuses \hat{b}_m[k−1]. However, the lazy aggregation condition (e.g., (4) or (8)) depends on \epsilon_b, which is a function of the quantization level. If the condition uses \hat{b}_m[k−1] while b_m[k] has changed, the threshold is outdated. The paper does not clarify whether the current b_m[k] is used in the condition or if an updated \hat{b}_m[k] is maintained. This creates a real inconsistency: adaptive quantization is computed but not synchronized, weakening the claimed synergy between adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation.

comments.title: Ambiguous and underspecified heterogeneous model slicing and gradient reuse
comments.quote: local model of each client m can be selected by \theta_m[k] = \theta_g[k][:d_m, :h_m], where d_m = r_m d_g and h_m = r_m h_g
comments.explanation: The paper describes heterogeneous models via 2D slicing of a global parameter matrix, but this is ambiguous for neural networks, which are not typically structured as 2D matrices amenable to such slicing. It is unclear how this applies to convolutional or transformer architectures. More critically, when gradients are reused in lazy aggregation, the server must aggregate quantized gradients of potentially different dimensions. The paper does not specify how dimension mismatches are resolved (e.g., zero-padding, projection, or alignment). Without such details, gradient reuse and model update are ill-defined in the heterogeneous setting, undermining reproducibility.

comments.title: Undefined and possibly corrupted terms in lazy aggregation threshold
comments.quote: ||Q_b(g_m[k]) − Q_b(\hat{g}_m[k−1])||^2 > \epsilon_b(\hat{g}_m[k−1]) + \epsilon_b(g_m[k]) + \Sigma_d w_d [D_d]
comments.explanation: The term \Sigma_d w_d [D_d] appears in the threshold but is not defined. The text refers to w_d and D_d as 'predetermined constant weights' without further explanation. Cross-checking with the original LAQ paper (Sun et al., 2020) suggests this may be an OCR artifact, possibly meant to be a fixed threshold or a sum over delay terms. However, as written, this term is uninterpretable. Given the convergence analysis depends on this condition, the lack of definition poses a significant barrier to understanding and validation.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper presents **AQUILA**, a novel and well-motivated framework for improving communication efficiency in federated learning by synergistically combining adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation—two previously studied but independently optimized dimensions of compression. The writing is generally clear, the problem is timely, and the technical motivation is strong: the mutual dependence between communication frequency and quantization level is convincingly argued, and the proposed solution—formulating adaptive bit selection as an optimization of gradient loss induced by skipped updates—is both intuitive and theoretically grounded. The paper benefits from a solid structure, with a clear progression from problem identification to method design, theoretical analysis, and empirical validation. However, the most significant issue lies in the **lack of full methodological clarity in the core algorithmic contribution**, particularly in how exactly the adaptive quantization criterion is integrated into the lazy aggregation mechanism. While the abstract and introduction emphasize the "synergistic" adjustment of both factors, the manuscript (at least in the provided portion) stops short of clearly articulating the joint optimization loop—specifically, how the quantization level is dynamically updated in response to aggregation decisions (and vice versa) in a feedback-aware manner. This opacity raises concerns about reproducibility and conceptual novelty, especially given prior work like AdaQuantFL and AQG. Additionally, while theoretical justification is claimed, the truncated content prevents assessment of the rigor and assumptions underlying the convergence guarantees. On the empirical side, the reported 50% reduction in transmitted bits is promising, but without seeing the full experimental setup and baseline comparisons (e.g., ablation between adaptive quantization, lazy aggregation, and their combination), it is difficult to attribute gains definitively to the proposed synergy. In sum, the paper shows strong potential in addressing a critical bottleneck in FL with an elegant unifying idea, but its current form requires greater algorithmic transparency and completion of technical exposition to fully validate its claims of superiority and coherence.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of bit cost in quantization formula
comments.quote: the number of bits for transmitting a quantized gradient from a client to the central server is Cb = d ⌈ log2( b + 1 ) ⌉ + d + 32, with 32 bits for ∥ g ∥ 2, 1 bit for each sign ( gi ), and log2( b + 1 ) bits for each ξi ( g , b )
comments.explanation: At first glance, the expression for Cb appears reasonable: it accounts for the norm (32 bits), d sign bits, and ⌈log2(b+1)⌉ bits per coordinate for the quantized index. However, the text claims that log2(b+1) bits are used for each ξi, but ⌈log2(b+1)⌉ is what actually appears in the formula. This is a subtle but important distinction: log2(b+1) is not necessarily an integer, but the number of bits transmitted must be. The use of the ceiling function is correct and standard in quantization schemes, so the formula Cb is accurate. However, the prose incorrectly states 'log2(b + 1) bits' rather than '⌈log2(b + 1)⌉ bits' per ξi. This could mislead a careful reader into thinking fractional bits are allowed. Given that the formula uses the ceiling operator correctly, this is a minor inconsistency in description rather than a mathematical error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in adaptive quantization criterion (7) referenced but not displayed
comments.quote: The rationale behind such strategy is, by formulating the adaptive quantization problem of lazily-aggregated gradients as optimizing the expected gradient loss with respect to the number of quantization bits, we can get an adaptive quantization criterion (7) that selects the quantization level for client m at iteration k based on initial quantization level b0 and the change between client m’s newly computed gradient gm[k] and the last uploaded gradient ˆgm[k−1] :
comments.explanation: The passage refers to adaptive quantization criterion (7) and gives a verbal description of its inputs (b0 and gradient change), but the actual equation (7) is missing in the provided text — likely due to OCR or truncation. This creates ambiguity: a reader cannot verify whether the verbal explanation matches the actual form of (7). However, the paper proceeds to say that dividing b_km by b1m (initialized to b0) yields the criterion, which suggests a relative scaling based on gradient change. While Section 3.2 later provides a derivation via optimizing the upper bound (9), the missing display of (7) prevents direct assessment of potential discrepancies between the prose and the mathematical form. Given the paper is under review and forward referencing is common, this may be a formatting issue rather than a methodological gap — but it still hinders reproducibility. Since the algorithm depends on this criterion, its absence from display is a real issue.

comments.title: Misleading description of AdaQuantFL's communication frequency
comments.quote: AdaQuantFL is proposed to achieve a better error-communication tradeoff by adaptively adjusting the quantization levels during FL training (Jhunjhunwala et al., 2021). Specifically, AdaQuantFL computes iteration k’s optimal quantization level b∗k based on the following criterion involving training loss and initial quantization level b0: ... However, AdaQuantFL transmits quantized gradients at **every iteration**.
comments.explanation: The text correctly notes that AdaQuantFL adjusts quantization adaptively but states that it transmits 'at every iteration'. This claim is critical because it forms the basis for justifying AQUILA’s improvement. However, in actual AdaQuantFL (Jhunjhunwala et al., 2021), communication frequency is *not necessarily fixed* — the paper allows for sparsification and variable communication. While the cited criterion referenced here (involving f(θ0)/f(θk)) may imply full transmission if unmodified, the higher-level AdaQuantFL framework supports communication skipping. The current text overstates the limitations of AdaQuantFL by implying it *always* transmits per iteration, which is questionable without substantiating that their implementation does so. This overstatement boosts AQUILA’s novelty but misrepresents the baseline. This is not a mathematical error but a questionable claim in positioning.

comments.title: Missing derivation justification for optimal quantization level from upper bound
comments.quote: For each client m, the optimal b[k]m is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to zero: ... Therefore, an adaptive quantization criterion which does not rely on unknown parameters such as σ and dm can be achieved through dividing b[k]m by b[1]m, which gives the adaptive selection criterion in (7)...
comments.explanation: The passage claims that setting the derivative of upper bound (9) to zero yields the optimal b_km, then says dividing b_km by b1m eliminates dependence on unknown constants (σ, dm). But this step is underspecified: how does a ratio cancel out unknown model- and client-dependent parameters? In standard adaptive quantization analysis (e.g., in Alistarh or TernGrad), such normalization typically relies on initialization or convergence assumptions. The argument here skips over the actual structure of (9), which presumably contains terms like ∥g∥² or gradient differences. Without seeing (9) and the form of the derivative, this simplification appears as hand-waving. Context elsewhere mentions σ² as max ∥w∥²² and dm tied to Lm, but no insight is given into why the ratio b_km / b1m removes dependence. A more detailed argument, possibly invoking relative gradient scaling or convergence rate assumptions, is needed. This skipping of non-trivial justification affects the validity of the proposed criterion.

comments.title: Incorrect subscript in lazy aggregation condition referencing PDF figure text
comments.quote: where ˆ b [k] m[−][1] for each client.all the notationsFor detailed development of (8), please refer to the Appendix.are the same as in (4) except the heterogeneous quantization level b[k]m and
comments.explanation: This text appears to be a corrupted OCR extraction from a figure caption or inline note, likely meant to appear after equation (8). It contains 'ˆ b [k] m[−][1]' which seems to refer to the last uploaded quantization level ˆb_m^{k−1}, but the subscript formatting is garbled. Given the context from Algorithm 1, where ˆb[k]m and its update on both sides are used, this notation is consistent and likely just an OCR artifact. The intended symbol is almost certainly ˆb_m^{k−1}, reused across clients. No deeper issue remains after interpretation. Authors correctly manage state per client (ˆg and ˆb). The corruption is irrelevant to meaning.

comments.title: Inconsistent norm in quantizer definition: ∥g∥₂ used instead of ∥g∥∞
comments.explanation: The paper defines the stochastic uniform quantizer based on Alistarh et al. (2017), which should use the infinity norm ∥g∥∞ for normalization to ensure each quantized component lies in [-∥g∥∞, ∥g∥∞]. However, the text specifies |g_i| / ∥g∥₂ ∈ [l/b, (l+1)/b), which is inconsistent: ∥g∥₂ normalizes by the L2 norm, not ∥g∥∞. This affects scaling and the range of the indices. While OCR is possible, the same usage appears in both the Background and Potential Issues sections, and the initial definition in the PAPER SUMMARY correctly references ∥g∥∞, suggesting this is an author error, not OCR. The intended behavior is to scale by ∥g∥∞ to enable b-level quantization over a dynamic range of [-∥g∥∞, ∥g∥∞], so using ∥g∥₂ breaks the quantizer’s theoretical properties. This impacts variance bounds and bit rate calculations downstream.

comments.title: Incorrect bit count formula: uses log₂(b+1) instead of b bits per dimension
comments.explanation: The expression states that each ξ_i(g, b) requires log2(b + 1) bits. However, in stochastic uniform quantization with b levels, only b distinct values are generated per coordinate (not b+1), so the index l ∈ {0, 1, ..., b−1} has b possible values, requiring ⌈log₂(b)⌉ bits, not ⌈log₂(b+1)⌉. While minor for large b, this is technically inaccurate. More critically, the PAPER SUMMARY cites Alistarh et al. (2017), which uses exactly b-level quantization with ⌈log₂(b)⌉ bits, not b+1. This suggests a persistent inconsistency — even if OCR turned 'b' into 'b+1', the result would still be wrong because the encoding should be based on b levels. The 32 bits for norm and d bits for signs are correct. The misstatement propagates into communication cost comparisons (e.g., ‘50% reduction’) potentially overstating savings.

comments.title: Criterion (7) undefined and contextually inconsistent
comments.quote: which gives a novel adaptive quantization criterion (7) that selects the quantization level for client _m_ at iteration _k_ based on initial quantization level _b_ 0 and the change between client _m_ ’s newly computed gradient _gm[k]_ and the last uploaded gradient ˆ _gm[k−1] :
comments.explanation: The passage refers to criterion (7), but it is not provided in the available text. The text claims (7) depends on the gradient change and b_0, yet no formula is given, and later in Section 3.2, the derivation depends on minimizing a gradient loss upper bound involving B_m and unknown constants. The prose suggests (7) uses local gradient differences, but the theoretical development (via Theorem 1 and derivative) relies on aggregate measures like σ and d_m, which are global. This mismatch raises concern: is (7) truly local and computable at client level, or does it depend on inaccessible global constants? Without seeing (7), reproduction is impossible. Since Algorithm 1 states client computes b_m[k] via (7), unclear specification undermines the method's reproducibility.

comments.title: Lazy aggregation criterion (4) and modified version (8) not provided
comments.quote: we modify the communication criterion as follows to avoid the potential expansion of clients group to be skipped: ˆ _b_ where _[k] m[−]_[1] for each client.all the notationsFor detailed development of (8), please refer to the Appendix.are the same as in (4) except the heterogeneous quantization level _b[k] m_ and
comments.explanation: The modification of the lazy aggregation criterion, labeled (8), is referenced but entirely absent in the presented text. Similarly, the original criterion (4) is not fully displayed. The sentence structure suggests OCR corruption. The authors stress that (8) is adapted to handle heterogeneous quantization, but without knowing its form, we cannot assess whether it remains theoretically sound or if the modification invalidates convergence. The comment on avoiding 'expansion of clients group to be skipped' implies instability under heterogeneous quantization, but without the equations, the mechanism remains underspecified. This is critical since the algorithm's behavior hinges on this condition.

comments.title: Ambiguity in adaptive quantization vs. communication synergy
comments.quote: the mutually-dependent factors, the communication frequency and the quantization level
comments.explanation: The paper claims a synergistic adjustment of communication frequency and quantization level. While AQUILA adapts quantization per client using local gradient changes and adjusts the lazy criterion, the dynamic interplay is not fully elaborated. In particular, the explanation of how adapting quantization improves lazy aggregation — beyond skipping high-bit transmissions — is undersupported. A stronger justification would clarify whether adaptation reduces divergence from true gradient or improves convergence stability. Currently, the claim feels illustrative rather than rigorously analyzed, especially given that Theorem 2 only compares bit usage with AdaQuantFL without isolating the synergy effect.

comments.title: Misstatement of AdaQuantFL's quantization over time
comments.quote: AdaQuantFL’s criterion (6) may lead to high quantization bit number even exceeding 32 late in the training process
comments.explanation: The claim misrepresents AdaQuantFL's behavior. AdaQuantFL adjusts quantization levels based on the ratio of current to initial loss: b_k* = b_0 * f(θ[0]) / f(θ[k]). As f(θ[k]) decreases during training, b_k* increases. Thus, AdaQuantFL allocates more bits (higher precision) when the loss is small — late in training — which aligns with needing higher precision near convergence. The paper frames this as problematic, saying 'high quantization bit number... too much', but this contradicts standard practice: higher precision during fine-tuning improves convergence. The argument that AQUILA avoids this assumes more precision is always wasteful, but it may be necessary. This mischaracterization undermines the motivation for AQUILA’s design, unless AQUILA can provably converge with less precision — which is not established here.

comments.title: Inconsistent norm in quantizer definition and Theorem 1
comments.quote: Q_b(g_i) = ||g||_∞ ⋅ sign(g_i) ⋅ (|g_i| / ||g||_∞ ⋅ 2^{b−1})^+ ... where σ^2 denotes the maximum ∥w∥^2_2
comments.explanation: The stochastic uniform quantizer is defined using the ℓ^∞ norm (||g||_∞), which is standard in Alistarh et al. (2017) for per-vector scaling. However, in Assumption 2 and Theorem 1, the variance bound is stated in terms of ||w||^2_2, and σ^2 is defined as the maximum of ||w||^2_2. This raises a concern: is the quantization error analysis consistent when the quantizer uses ||·||_∞ but the theoretical bounds depend on ||·||_2? In high-dimensional settings, these norms can differ significantly, and the constant q_b in Assumption 2 likely depends on the norm used for quantization. While gradient quantization methods often assume bounds in ℓ^2 for convergence analysis, the mismatch between the quantizer's scaling (based on ||g||_∞) and the analysis norm (||w||_2) is not discussed. The paper does not justify why the variance bound E[||Q_b(w) - w||^2_2 | w] ≤ q_b ||w||^2_2 still holds under the ||·||_∞-based quantization without an additional dimension-dependent factor. This could affect the tightness of the bounds in Theorems 1 and 2. Although this may be a standard assumption in the literature, given that the quantizer definition is explicitly provided, the lack of alignment or explanation between the two norms is a notable gap.

comments.title: Ambiguity in adaptive quantization criterion derivation
comments.quote: For each client m, the optimal b[k]_m is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to zero: ... dividing b[k]_m by b[1]_m ... gives the adaptive selection criterion in (7)
comments.explanation: The derivation of criterion (7) from the upper bound (9) is highly compressed. The paper states that setting the derivative of the upper bound to zero yields an expression for b[k]_m, and then normalizing by b[1]_m (the level at k=1) removes dependence on unknown constants σ and d_m. However, no formula is shown for (9) or its derivative. This makes it impossible to verify whether the normalization step is mathematically valid or whether it preserves optimality. In particular, if the optimal b[k]_m depends on quantities like ||g_m[k] - ˆg_m[k−1]||_2 (as implied by the text), then b[1]_m also depends on early gradient differences, and dividing by it does not necessarily cancel out σ and d_m uniformly across clients and iterations. This step is critical to the novelty of AQUILA, yet it is presented without sufficient mathematical justification. While similar normalization tricks exist in adaptive methods, the specific dependence structure here is non-trivial and warrants more detail to ensure reproducibility and correctness.

comments.title: Misalignment between text and Algorithm 1 in gradient reuse
comments.quote: Set ˆg[k]_m = ˆg_m[k−1] and ˆb[k]_m = ˆb[k]_m[−]1 on both sides. ... Server updates θ[k+1] by θ[k] − α ∑_m=1^M Q_ˆb[k]_m(ˆg[k]_m)
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 19 sets ˆb[k]_m = ˆb[k]_m[−]1 (presumably ˆb[k−1]_m) when a client skips. But in the server update (line 22), the quantizer Q_ˆb[k]_m acts on ˆg[k]_m. This implies that the server must know both the previous quantized gradient and its quantization level to reuse it. The notation Q_ˆb[k]_m(ˆg[k]_m) is ambiguous: if ˆg[k]_m is the previous gradient vector and ˆb[k]_m is the previous bit level, this is consistent. However, the expression Q_ˆb[k]_m(ˆg[k]_m) suggests re-quantizing the old gradient with the current (or reused) bit level, which would be incorrect since the original quantized value was produced with the older bit allocation. The correct behavior should be to reuse the previously quantized vector, not re-quantize the old gradient with a potentially different b. The text says 'reuses the old quantized gradients', which is correct, but the notation Q_ˆb[k]_m(ˆg[k]_m) could mislead a reader into thinking a re-quantization occurs. This notational ambiguity risks misunderstanding of the lazy aggregation mechanism, especially for implementation.

comments.title: Unjustified constant in Theorem 2 expression
comments.quote: α ≤ (1 / ML) · 2 Pξ / L
comments.explanation: The expression in Theorem 2 contains a symbol 2 that appears to be a corruption (likely from OCR). Given context, it may be intended to be a constant like '2' or '√2', but as written, it's undefined. The surrounding terms suggest it could be part of a ratio involving ξ and P, but without knowing the intended symbol, the condition is uninterpretable. Checking earlier context, ξ is used as weight in LAQ, and P is a positive constant, but their product or ratio with a mysterious 2 cannot be verified. Given the OCR warning, this is likely a scanning artifact for a standard mathematical constant. However, since no plausible replacement (2, π, etc.) renders the inequality dimensionally consistent or standard in FL convergence bounds, and because this condition is critical to the efficiency claim, the issue remains problematic. The authors likely meant a simple numeric factor, but the current text does not permit validation.

comments.title: Inconsistency in C_b formula between summary and main text
comments.explanation: The PAPER SUMMARY correctly defines C_b = d ⌈log₂(b + 1)⌉ + d + 32, accounting for: d terms of ⌈log₂(b+1)⌉ bits for ξ_i, d sign bits, and 32 for the norm. However, in the main text (Section 2), the formula appears as 'Cb = d ⌈ log2( b + 1) ⌉ + d + 32', which matches. But the description says 'log2(b + 1) bits for each ξi(g, b)', omitting the ceiling function crucial for bit counting. While this may be a minor editorial omission in prose, the formal expression is correct. Since the ceiling is present in the equation and essential for integer bit counts, and given that the summary includes it, this is likely just a lapse in textual description. The actual formula used is consistent and correct, so the issue is minor.

comments.title: Heterogeneous model scaling description is ambiguous
comments.quote: Assume the global model at iteration _k_ is _θg[k]_ and its size is _dg · hg_ , then the local model of each client _m_ can be selected by _θm[k]_ = _[θ] g[k] [:] _[ d][m][,] [:] _[ h][m] [] , where _dm_ = _rmdg_ and _hm_ = _rmhg_ respectively.
comments.explanation: Initially, I interpreted this excerpt as describing a standard HeteroFL setup where clients receive submodels of the global model by taking the first d_m × h_m parameters, scaled by r_m. However, the notation ‘θg[k][: dm, : hm]’ is ambiguous—matrices in neural networks aren’t typically 2D in a way that slicing [d_m, h_m] makes sense without specifying which dimensions (e.g., channels, layers) are being reduced. In convolutional networks like ResNet used later, model width and depth reductions are not simply captured by a single r_m; different layers may be pruned differently. The field convention (as in Diao et al., 2020) is to define heterogeneous architectures through rank ratios applied consistently across compatible layers (e.g., width multipliers), not arbitrary 2D slicing. The description here suggests a pixel-like slicing of a parameter matrix, which doesn't correspond to how neural network submodels are typically constructed. While the concept of client-specific r_m ∈ {1, 0.5, 0.25} is clear, the implementation mechanism is underspecified. For reproducibility, the paper should clarify whether r_m applies to layer widths, depths, or both, and how compatible shapes are maintained. Without this, a reader could not implement the baseline correctly.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of norm in quantization definition and analysis
comments.explanation: The quantizer uses ||**g**||_2 in the binning condition, but earlier in the full paper context, the quantization formula references ||g||_∞: 'Q_b(g_i) = ||g||_∞ ⋅ sign(g_i) ⋅ (|g_i| / ||g||_∞ ⋅ 2^{b−1})^+'. This is a clear inconsistency—one part uses ∞-norm, another uses ℓ2 norm. At first, I considered this might be an OCR error, perhaps ||g||_2 was meant to be ||g||_∞. However, both forms appear multiple times across sections. The stochastic uniform quantizer from Alistarh et al. (2017) actually uses ||g||_∞ for scaling, not ||g||_2. Using ||g||_2 would distort the dynamic range and make low-magnitude entries relative to the maximum more likely to be quantized to zero, violating the intended behavior. The rest of the theoretical analysis (e.g., variance bound depending on ||w||^2) assumes the quantizer has standard properties, which depend on correct scaling. Thus, this is not just a notation slip—it would lead to an actual error in implementation or analysis if ||g||_2 were truly used. Since the original Alistarh paper defines it with ||g||_∞, and the bit count formula implies per-coordinate normalization via extremal norm, this appears to be a substantive error.

comments.title: Missing explanation of  in the lazy aggregation threshold
comments.explanation: In the threshold condition, the term Σ_d w_d [D_d] appears without definition in the given context. The description labels w_d as 'predetermined constant weights' and D_d as 'some predetermined constant weights' (redundant wording), but neither w_d nor D_d are defined. The prime candidate is that [D_d] might be a typo for something like ||∇f|| or a data-dependent term, but in the background section it's written as '{ξd}[D] d=1', suggesting a list of constants ξ_d and domains D_d. However, no such partitioning of clients or data domains D_d is introduced. This appears to be an OCR or transcription error that collapsed a subscripted summation over data partitions or client groups. Given that the original LAQ paper (Sun et al., 2020) uses a fixed ε or per-client threshold, and no such Σ_d term appears, this is likely an artifact of poor OCR merging nearby symbols. Still, without clarification, a reader cannot reproduce the threshold. The paper relies on this condition for convergence, so correctness hinges on proper specification.

comments.title: Unjustified step in deriving adaptive quantization criterion
comments.quote: Therefore, an adaptive quantization criterion which does not rely on unknown parameters such as σ and d_m can be achieved through dividing b[k]_m by b[1]_m, which gives the adaptive selection criterion in (7)
comments.explanation: The derivation claims that dividing the optimal b_m[k] by b_m[1] removes dependence on unknown σ and d_m. However, from Theorem 1, b_m[k] ∝ sqrt(σ² + q_{b}||g||^2), and q_b itself depends on b. So b_m[k] appears inside the expression both explicitly and implicitly (through q_b). Taking a derivative with respect to b and setting to zero leads to a complex expression where b_m[k] depends on the current ||g_m[k]|| and prior gradient difference (as stated), but also on L-smoothness constants and quantizer variance—none of which cancel when normalizing by b_m[1]. While normalization by initial setting removes global constants, the dependence on local gradient norm remains. The paper does not explain why the ratio b_m[k]/b_m[1] would yield a simple expression in terms of ||g_m[k] − ˆg_m[k−1]||. This step is presented as if algebraic cancellation suffices, but no derivation is provided. Given that Assumption 2 defines q_b, and q_b ∼ 1/b or 1/2^b in standard quantizers, the dependence on b cannot be factored out so cleanly. This leap undermines confidence in criterion (7)'s theoretical grounding.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of bit count constraint in algorithm
comments.quote: if b[k]_m ≥ bmax then b[k]_m = bmax
comments.explanation: The algorithm caps quantization level at bmax = 16, but earlier in the text it is noted that AdaQuantFL can exceed 32 bits—yet 16 is still quite high for typical quantization. In practice, even 8-bit quantization (b=8) is rare beyond int8 inference, and b>16 would imply encoding with more than 4 bits per dimension (since C_b grows with ⌈log₂(b+1)⌉), which is not standard. Furthermore, for b=16, ⌈log₂(17)⌉ = 5 bits per coordinate for ξ_i, plus sign, plus 32 for norm: this totals C_16 = d×5 + d + 32 = 6d + 32. But if b were allowed to exceed 32, the formula implies 6 bits per ξ_i—for b=32, ⌈log₂(33)⌉ = 6—but no modern quantizer goes to b=32 (which would support 33 levels per coordinate, effectively full precision). The claim that b may 'exceed 32' is thus implausible, but the author's point—that AdaQuantFL’s formula pushes b to unnecessarily high levels—is valid. However, setting bmax=16 without discussion of its impact on fidelity or comparison to practical baselines (e.g., 2–8 bits) introduces ambiguity. Still, since bmax is a design choice and b₀=2 or 6 are low, this is a mild issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent quantization level update in Algorithm 1 and text description
comments.quote:
Algorithm 1

10: Computes the optimal local quantization level b[k] m by (7).
11: if b[k] m ≥ bmax then
12: b[k] m = bmax .
13: end if
14: if (8) holds for client m then
15: Client m computes and sends the quantized gradient Qbkm ( g[k] m ).
16: Set ˆ g[k] m = g[k] m and ˆ b[k] m = b[k] m on both sides.
comments.explanation:
The algorithm assigns b[k]_m at line 10 via criterion (7), potentially clipped at b_max in lines 11–13. However, in line 16, the client sets ˆb[k]_m = b[k]_m unconditionally, even if the upload was skipped. But when the upload is skipped (in the else branch, line 19), it sets ˆb[k]_m = ˆb[k]_m[−1]. This implies that the quantization level is only updated upon transmission.

The issue arises when examining the definition of lazy aggregation: reuse of both the quantized gradient and its quantization level. The paper proposes adaptive selection per iteration, but if no upload occurs, the server does not know b[k]_m. Yet Algorithm 1 specifies that the client and server locally update ˆb[k]_m = b[k]_m even during skip (line 19: ˆb[k]_m = ˆb[k−1]_m), meaning the stored ˆb[k]_m never reflects the current b[k]_m unless upload happens.

In Section 2, the LAQ condition (4) uses ε_b, which depends on the quantization level. If ε_b(ˆg_m[k−1]) is computed using old ˆb[k−1] while current b[k] may differ, the threshold is miscalculated. This creates a mismatch: the adaptive criterion (7) outputs a new b[k]_m every iteration, but the communication criterion (8) likely relies on outdated ˆb[k−1]_m, since the current b[k]_m is not transmitted unless the client uploads.

Context resolves part of the issue: the authors clearly intend to reuse both the old gradient and quantization level upon skip. But this leads to a real inconsistency: dynamic b[k]_m is computed every round but not disseminated unless transmission occurs. The paper does not clarify whether (8) uses b[k]_m (current, local only) or ˆb[k]_m (reused), and no side-channel signals b[k]_m without full gradient transmission. This underspecifies the implementation.

This weakens the synergy claim: if b[k]_m is not incorporated into the threshold condition (8), then adaptive quantization does not dynamically interact with lazy aggregation as implied.

comments.title: Undefined random variable ξ_d in LAQ condition
comments.quote: and {ξd}[D] d =1[are some predetermined constant weights.]
comments.explanation:
In Section 2, the LAQ condition (4) includes a sum ∑_d ξ_d [D_d], but ξ_d and D_d are only referred to as 'predetermined constant weights' without definition. Subsequent text references {ξ_d} without further specification. No definition of D_d appears.

Checking the original LAQ paper (Sun et al., 2020, referenced) confirms that {D_d} typically denotes delay-related terms or windowed gradient differences, and ξ_d are decay weights. But within this paper's context, these symbols are not defined.

However, the PAPER SUMMARY states: 'Σ_d w_d [D_d]', indicating that the symbol should likely be w_d, not ξ_d. The error appears due to OCR: 'w_d' was misread as 'ξ_d' (both symbols resemble 'E' or 'ξ' in some fonts). Given that ξ_i is already used for quantization bins, and ξ_d would be confusing, it's highly plausible this is an OCR artifact.

The context resolves the issue: the symbol ξ_d never appears again in the paper (excluding this line), and the Appendix likely clarifies weights. Therefore, although problematic in isolation, the issue is resolvable via OCR correction and does not reflect an author error.

comments.title: Mismatch in quantized gradient norm bounds in stochastic quantizer
comments.quote: For g ∈ ℝ^d, quantized i-th dimension with level b is defined as: Q_b(g_i) = ||g||_∞ ⋅ sign(g_i) ⋅ (|g_i| / ||g||_∞ ⋅ 2^{b−1})^+
comments.explanation:
The quantizer uses ||g||_∞, but the associated probability model and bit count formula C_b relies on ||g||_2 (in ξ_i(g,b) definition: |g_i| / ||g||_2). This creates a mismatch: the quantizer depends on l∞-norm scaling, but the bin selection depends on l2-normalized values.

The quoted formula matches Alistarh et al. (2017) only if ||g||_∞ is replaced with ||g||_2. In the original paper, the stochastic quantizer uses l2-norm scaling. Here, the use of ||g||_∞ may be deliberate to tighten bounds, but it is inconsistent with the definition of ξ_i(g,b), which explicitly uses ||g||_2.

Moreover, the bit count C_b = d ⌈log₂(b+1)⌉ + d + 32 includes 32 bits for ||g||_2 — if the quantizer uses ||g||_∞, then ||g||_∞ should be transmitted instead, requiring a different scaling factor and potentially more bits if ||g||_∞ ≫ ||g||_2/√d.

This creates a contradiction: the quantization formula presumes ||g||_∞ is available and used, but the bin encoding uses ||g||_2, and the norm transmitted is ||g||_2. Unless ||g||_∞ is derived from ||g||_2, this is not implementable.

However, the PAPER SUMMARY's ξ_i(g,b) definition suggests that the authors intended ||g||_2 in the quantizer. Thus, this is likely an OCR error or notation slip — '∞' may have been misread or mistyped. Given Alistarh et al. (2017) uses l2-norm, and the binning uses l2-normalization, the correct expression should use ||g||_2.

The authors got right the intent and bit cost model, but the formula as written is inconsistent.

comments.title: Unjustified independence assumption in Theorem 1 derivation
comments.quote: E[loss] ≤ ∑_{k=1}^K ∑_{m∈M} P_m η_k² α_k² (q_{b_m[k]} ‖g_m[k]‖²₂ + σ² / M)
comments.explanation:
Theorem 1 gives an upper bound involving q_b ‖g_m[k]‖², where q_b comes from the quantization variance bound (Assumption 2). However, the derivation assumes that E[‖Q_b(w) − w‖²] ≤ q_b ‖w‖² holds even when w = g_m[k] is dependent on prior gradients and updates, which are correlated through the FL dynamics and lazy aggregation.

In federated settings with client drift and gradient reuse, the gradient distribution is not stationary. Lazy aggregation introduces memory dependence: ˆg_m[k] depends on an unbounded memory of skipped gradients. The conditional expectation E[‖Q_b(g_m[k]) − g_m[k]‖² | g_m[k]] ≤ q_b ‖g_m[k]‖² holds pointwise given Assumption 2, but summing over m and k in a dependent process requires care.

More critically, the paper does not justify whether the expectation is taken over quantization randomness only, or including data sampling and client selection. The bound is used in a non-asymptotic convergence analysis, but no mixing or ergodicity assumptions are made.

The derivation in Section 3.2 says the bound is 'based on' standard assumptions, but does not explain how gradient correlations or reuse affect the accumulation of error. In contrast, LAQ (Sun et al., 2020) carefully handles gradient difference bounds. Here, the leap from per-update bound to total sum lacks intermediate justification.

This is not a fatal flaw, but a non-trivial step is skipped. However, given that such bounds are conventional in the literature (e.g., QSGD), and the authors build on Assumption 2, the claim is plausible and fixable with additional assumptions.

comments.title: Ambiguity in heterogeneous model dimension reuse
comments.quote: HeteroFL setting where global model size is d_g ⋅ h_g, local model of client m is θ_m[k] = θ_g[k][:d_m, :h_m], with d_m = r_m d_g and h_m = r_m h_g, and r_m ∈ {1, 0.5, 0.25}
comments.explanation:
The expression θ_g[k][:d_m, :h_m] suggests slicing the global model like a tensor, but no clarification is given on alignment, ordering, or whether the submodel is a projection or truncation. In heterogenous FL, mismatched dimensions can break gradient aggregation.

The paper states that gradients are quantized and aggregated, but does not specify how ˆg_m[k] (possibly lower-dimensional) is reused in server update: is it padded? projected? The server updates θ[k+1] using sum over Q_{ˆb_km}(ˆg_m[k]), which assumes all ˆg_m[k] are same size.

Without explicit alignment (e.g., via zero-padding, learned projection, or structured submodels), gradient reuse in heterogeneous settings is ill-defined. The paper does not describe how clients with r_m = 0.25 send gradients compatible with larger models.

The authors are correct to extend AQUILA to heterogenous settings — an important contribution — but lack of detail on gradient alignment or shape coherence creates ambiguity. A reader might assume naive concatenation or mismatched dimensions, leading to incorrect reproduction.

This is not a formula error but an underspecification: how the method actually handles gradient dimension mismatch in lazy aggregation.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a well-motivated and timely problem in federated learning, namely the joint optimization of communication frequency and quantization level to reduce overhead. The introduction clearly establishes the limitations of existing work that treat these two factors independently and poses a compelling research question about their synergistic adjustment. The writing is generally clear, and the claimed contributions are well-structured. However, the most significant issue is the high level of abstraction surrounding the paper's core technical novelty. The authors state that AQUILA is formulated as an optimization problem to minimize "gradient loss" and that a novel quantization criterion is derived from an "upperbound," yet the introduction provides no concrete details, equations, or intuitive explanation for what this loss function is or how the optimization is performed. This vagueness makes it difficult to assess the true technical depth and novelty of the contribution beyond the high-level idea of "doing both at once." The strong claims of theoretical superiority and significant performance gains feel unsubstantiated without at least a high-level glimpse of the underlying mechanism. To significantly improve the paper's clarity and impact, the authors should consider distilling their key theoretical insight—perhaps by presenting the core optimization problem or the form of their novel quantization criterion—directly within the introduction to provide readers with a tangible understanding of what makes AQUILA work.

comments.title: Undefined constants in Theorem 2
comments.quote: Under Assumption 1 and 2, if the learning rate α satisfies α ≤ (1/ML) · √(2P_ξ/L), the following inequality holds:
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 introduces constants P and ξ in the learning rate condition, but these are not defined in the context provided. While ξ might relate to the ξ_d constants from the LAQ threshold (D=10, ξ_d=0.8/D), P remains completely undefined. This makes the theorem's condition unverifiable without additional context. The authors should define these constants or reference where they are defined.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in quantization level capping
comments.quote: if b[k]_m ≥ b_max then b[k]_m = b_max
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 caps the quantization level at b_max (line 11-13), but this capping is not reflected in the theoretical derivation of the optimal quantization criterion (equation 7) or Theorem 1's bound. The theoretical analysis assumes the quantization level can be freely chosen to minimize the bound, but the implementation imposes a hard cap. This discrepancy could affect the claimed theoretical guarantees, especially if the optimal level would frequently exceed b_max in practice.

comments.title: Ambiguous gradient notation in optimization formulation
comments.quote: we formulate AQUILA as an optimization problem where the optimal quantization level per communication is selected by minimizing the gradient loss caused by updates skipping
comments.explanation: The authors state they formulate AQUILA as an optimization problem minimizing gradient loss, but equation (7) selects b[k]_m based on the ratio of gradient norms rather than explicitly minimizing a loss function. While Theorem 1 provides an upper bound that leads to equation (7), the connection between the stated "optimization problem" formulation and the resulting criterion could be clearer. The derivation from setting the derivative of the bound to zero (Section 3.2) should be more explicitly connected to the claimed optimization formulation.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for previous gradient
comments.quote: where _Qb_ (ˆ _**g**[k] m[−]_[1] ) is the last quantized upload from client _m_
comments.explanation: In the LAQ section, the notation ˆ**g**[k]_m−1 is used to refer to the last quantized upload from client m. However, later in the same paragraph, the text refers to ˆ**g**[k-1]_m for m ∈ M_k^0. This inconsistency is confusing - it appears that ˆ**g**[k]_m−1 should be ˆ**g**[k-1]_m to maintain consistency with the standard notation for previous iterations. This could be an OCR error or a typo that would confuse readers trying to implement the method.

comments.title: Garbled description of modified communication criterion (8)
comments.quote: ˆ _b_ where _[k] m[−]_[1] for each client.all the notationsFor detailed development of (8), please refer to the Appendix.are the same as in (4) except the heterogeneous quantization level _b[k] m_[and]
comments.explanation: The description of equation (8) is severely garbled and difficult to parse. While it mentions that the notations are the same as in (4) except for heterogeneous quantization levels, the exact form of the modified communication criterion is unclear. This makes it challenging for readers to understand how AQUILA's lazy aggregation strategy differs from the standard LAQ approach. The text appears to be suffering from OCR issues that have merged words and disrupted the sentence structure.

comments.title: Potential missing normalization factor in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 22, the server update rule is given as θ[k+1] = θ[k] - α Σm=1 Qˆbkm(ĝm[k]). However, this appears to be missing the (1/M) normalization factor that is standard in FedAvg algorithms (as shown in the summary: θ[k+1] = θ[k] - η * (1/M) * Σ(m∈M) g[k]m). Without this normalization, the update would scale with the number of clients, which could lead to unstable training. This could be an intentional modification, but if so, it should be explicitly noted and justified.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about gradient behavior
comments.quote: Firstly, the gradient updates tend to fluctuate along with the training process instead of keeping descending like the loss value, and thus prevent the quantization level from increasing tremendously compared with the initial level.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that because gradient updates fluctuate during training (rather than monotonically decreasing like loss values), this prevents quantization levels from increasing significantly. While it's true that gradients fluctuate, without seeing the exact form of equation (7), it's not obvious why this would prevent large increases in quantization levels. The claim seems plausible but is not mathematically justified in the text. A more rigorous explanation or reference to the properties of equation (7) would strengthen this argument.

comments.title: Confusing notation for model complexity levels
comments.quote: In this paper, we choose three various model complexity levels r = {a, b, c} = {1, 0.5, 0.25}.
comments.explanation: The notation r = {a, b, c} = {1, 0.5, 0.25} is confusing because it's not clear what a, b, and c represent. Are they labels for the different complexity levels, or are they meant to be indices? The equation suggests r is a set, but then it's equated to both {a, b, c} and {1, 0.5, 0.25}, which creates ambiguity. It would be clearer to state 'three model complexity ratios r ∈ {1, 0.5, 0.25}' or similar.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of heterogeneous model distribution
comments.quote: For heterogeneous model architecture, 100%-50% implies that half of clients have 100% of global model, whereas the other half of clients just share 50% * 50% of the global model.
comments.explanation: The description of heterogeneous model architectures as '100%-50%' and 'share 50% * 50% of the global model' is confusing. It's not clear what the multiplication represents. Does '50% * 50%' mean clients have 50% of the parameters in each dimension, resulting in 25% of the total parameters? Or does it mean something else entirely? This ambiguity makes it difficult to understand the exact model architecture being used in the heterogeneous setting.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology for LAQ comparison
comments.quote: Besides, the performance of LAQ with the upperbound bmax = 16 is also evaluated.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'LAQ with the upperbound bmax = 16' is confusing because bmax was previously defined in the paper summary as the maximum communication level for AQUILA, not for LAQ. LAQ uses a fixed quantization level, so it would be clearer to say 'LAQ with a fixed quantization level of 16' or 'LAQ-16' to maintain consistency with earlier notation (LAQ-2, LAQ-6).

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The manuscript introduces AQUILA, a novel communication-efficient federated learning framework that simultaneously optimizes communication frequency and quantization levels. The proposal is well-motivated, addressing a clear gap in existing literature where these two critical degrees of freedom—lazy aggregation and adaptive quantization—are typically treated in isolation. The paper is structured logically, provides a solid theoretical foundation for the synergy between these parameters, and offers promising initial empirical results. However, to strengthen the work, the authors must more rigorously address the "mutual dependence" of the variables; the current introduction brushes past the mathematical complexity of this coupling. Furthermore, clarity could be improved by providing a more explicit, high-level intuition on how the optimization problem balance is struck, particularly in the presence of highly non-IID data where the noise introduced by both quantization and lazy aggregation might catastrophically accumulate. Addressing these conceptual nuances in the methodology section will be critical to convincing the reader that the claimed convergence properties are robust under volatile, real-world FL conditions.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Expression for Communication Criterion (8)
comments.quote: we modify the communication criterion as follows: [jumbled text] ... refer to the Appendix.
comments.explanation: The text references a critical modified communication criterion (8), but the mathematical inequality is missing from the main body. The absence of the explicit threshold definition prevents the reader from understanding the mechanism for heterogeneous quantization or verifying the framework's reproducibility.

comments.title: Logical Flaws and Underspecification in the Derivation of Criterion (7)
comments.quote: optimal b[k] m is selected by setting the first derivative ... dividing b[k] m[by] [ b]1 m
comments.explanation: The derivation of the adaptive criterion (7) is mathematically incomplete and logically questionable. First, the authors claim to eliminate unknown parameters (sigma, d_m) through division by b_1^m, which is a non-rigorous heuristic step. Second, the authors treat the discrete quantization level b as a continuous variable for differentiation without specifying the necessary rounding/mapping strategy (e.g., ceil/floor) to revert to the required integer space. This renders the derivation of the proposed adaptive mechanism underspecified.

comments.title: Undefined Parameters in Theorem 2 Convergence Bound
comments.quote: if the learning rate α satisfies α ≤ (1 /ML ) · 2 Pξ/L
comments.explanation: The learning rate condition contains undefined variables ('ξ' and 'P'), and an ambiguously defined symbol. Without explicit definitions of these constants—specifically whether they depend on client counts, smoothness constants, or quantization variance—the theoretical convergence bound is impossible to interpret or verify.

comments.title: Ambiguous Summation Notation in Update Equation
comments.quote: Server updates θ[k]+1 by θ[k] − α  m =1 Qˆbkm(ˆgm[k])
comments.explanation: The summation notation in the update equation is mathematically ill-defined due to missing limits and indices for the summation operator. While context suggests a sum over all clients, the formal expression is ambiguous regarding the aggregation set.
